question: 11. Find the equation of the parabola which touches the \( x \)-axis at the point \( (1,0) \) and the \( y \)-axis at the point \( (0,2) \). Find the equation of the axis of the parabola and the coordinates of its vertex.
solution:
First Solution. Clearly the required parabola does not pass through the origin, and any conic not passing through the origin has an equation of the form
\[
A x^{2}+B x y+C y^{2}+D x+E y+1=0 .
\]

In order that this conic be tangent to the \( x \)-axis at \( (1,0) \), the equation obtained by setting \( y=0 \) must have a double root at \( x=1 \). Hence \( A=1 \) and \( D=-2 \). In order that it be tangent to the \( y \) axis at \( (0,2) \), we must have \( C=\frac{1}{4}, E=-1 \). In order that the conic be a parabola we must have \( B^{2}=4 A C \). This leads to two possibilities
\[
\begin{array}{l}
x^{2}+x y+\frac{1}{4} y^{2}-2 x-y+1=0 \\
x^{2}-x y+\frac{1}{4} y^{2}-2 x-y+1=0
\end{array}
\]

Since (1) can be written as \( \left(x+\frac{1}{2} y-1\right)^{2}=0 \), we see that it represents a degenerate conic, the double line through the two given points. Hence (2) is the equation of the required parabola.

It is convenient to multiply equation (2) by 4 to eliminate the fraction. We obtain
(3)
\[
4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2}-8 x-4 y+4=0
\]

Since the quadratic terms in (3) can be written in the form \( (2 x-y)^{2} \), a transformation is suggested. The orthogonal (but not scale-preserving) transformation
\[
\begin{array}{l}
u=2 x-y \\
v=x+2 y
\end{array}
\]
with inverse
\[
\begin{array}{l}
x=\frac{1}{5}(2 u+v) \\
y=\frac{1}{5}(-u+2 v)
\end{array}
\]
transforms (3) into
\[
u^{2}-\frac{12}{5} u-\frac{16}{5} v+4=0
\]

This has the standard form
\[
\left(u-\frac{6}{5}\right)^{2}-\frac{16}{5}\left(v-\frac{4}{5}\right)=0
\]

In this form the axis of the parabola is the line \( u=\frac{6}{5} \), and the vertex has \( u v \)-coordinates \( \left(\frac{6}{5}, \frac{4}{5}\right) \). In terms of the original coordinates, the axis has the equation \( 2 x-y=\frac{6}{5} \) and the vertex is at \( \left(\frac{16}{25}, \frac{2}{25}\right) \).

There is another way to handle the first part of this solution.
Suppose two distinct conics are given by the quadratic equations \( f(x, y) \) \( =0 \) and \( g(x, y)=0 \). These conics meet in four points (in the complex projective place, counting multiplicities) and any other conic passing through these four points has an equaiion \( \lambda f+\mu g=0 \) for suitable choice of \( \lambda \) and \( \mu \) (only the ratio \( \lambda: \mu \) matters, of course). Conics tangent to the \( x \)-axis at \( P \) and tangent to the \( \boldsymbol{y} \)-axis at \( Q \) form such a family because \( P \) and \( Q \) are both double points in this case. Two degenerate conics in this family are \( x y=0 \) (the union of the coordinate axes) and \( (2 x+y-2)^{2} \) \( =0 \) (the double line \( P Q \) ); consequently all conics of the family have equations of the form
\[
\lambda x y+\mu(2 x+y-2)^{2}=0 .
\]

Now we determine the parabola in this family by the condition that its discriminant vanishes. We find \( \lambda: \mu=0 \) or \( \lambda: \mu=-8 \). The former gives back the double line; and the latter gives the desired parabola.

Second Solution. Let \( \mathcal{P} \) be a parabola with focus \( F \) and directrix \( d \), and suppose two perpendicular lines through \( O \) are tangent to \( P \) at \( P \) and \( Q \), respectively. Using the well-known reflection property of the parabola, we shall prove synthetically that \( O \) is on \( d \) and \( F \) is the foot of the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \).

Let \( G \) and \( H \) be the reflections of \( F \) in the tangents \( O P \) and \( O Q \), respectively. Then triangles \( P O F \) and \( P O G \) are congruent, as are triangles \( Q O F \) and \( Q O H \). Because \( \angle F P O=\angle G P O \), the ray \( \overrightarrow{F P} \) on reflection from the parabola at \( P \) has the direction opposite to \( \overrightarrow{P G} \). From the reflection property it follows that \( P G \) is parallel to the axis of \( \mathcal{P} \) and perpendicular to \( d \). Therefore, the distance from \( P \) to \( d \) is measured along \( P G \). Since \( P \) is equidistant from \( F \) and \( d \) and \( P F=P G \), we conclude that \( G \) is on \( d \). Similarly, \( H \) is on \( d \).

Now \( \angle G O F=2 \angle P O F \) and \( \angle F O H=2 \angle F O Q \), so \( \angle G O H= \) \( 2 \angle P O Q=2 \) right angles, since \( O P \) and \( O Q \) are perpendicular. Hence \( O \) lies on \( G H=d \). Therefore \( \angle O G P \) and \( \angle O H Q \) are right angles. From the original congruent triangles it follows that \( \angle O F P \) and \( \angle O F Q \) are right angles. Hence \( \angle P F Q \) is a straight angle, so \( F \) is at the foot of the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \), as claimed.
In the problem at hand \( O \) is the origin and \( P \) and \( Q \) are the points \( (1,0) \) and ( 0,2 ). The line \( P Q \) has equation \( 2 x+y=2 \) and the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \) has slope \( \frac{1}{2} \). The point \( F \) is found to be \( \left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{2}{5}\right) \). The direction of the axis is found by reflecting the line \( P Q \) in either tangent, so the slope of the axis is 2 . Since it passes through \( F \) the equation of the axis is
\[
5 y=10 x-6
\]

The directrix \( \boldsymbol{d} \) has slope \( -\frac{1}{2} \) and passes through \( O \); hence its equation is \( 2 y+x=0 \). The directrix and axis meet at \( \left(\frac{12}{25}, \frac{6}{25}\right) \), and the vertex is halfway between this point and the focus; that is, at \( \left(\frac{16}{25}, \frac{2}{25}\right) \).

If \( (x, y) \) is any point on the parabola, it is equidistant from the directrix and the focus. Thus, using the squares of these distances
\[
\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^{2}=\left(x-\frac{4}{5}\right)^{2}+\left(y-\frac{2}{5}\right)^{2}
\]

This simplifies to
\[
4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2}-8 x-4 y+4=0,
\]
the equation of the parabola.

descriptive_long.question: Find the equation of the parabola which touches the \( abscissa \)-axis at the point \( (1,0) \) and the \( ordinate \)-axis at the point \( (0,2) \). Find the equation of the axis of the parabola and the coordinates of its vertex.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. Clearly the required parabola does not pass through the origin, and any conic not passing through the origin has an equation of the form
\[
consta\,abscissa^{2}+constb\,abscissa\,ordinate+constc\,ordinate^{2}+constd\,abscissa+conste\,ordinate+1=0 .
\]

In order that this conic be tangent to the \( abscissa \)-axis at \( (1,0) \), the equation obtained by setting \( ordinate=0 \) must have a double root at \( abscissa=1 \). Hence \( consta=1 \) and \( constd=-2 \). In order that it be tangent to the \( ordinate \) axis at \( (0,2) \), we must have \( constc=\frac{1}{4}, conste=-1 \). In order that the conic be a parabola we must have \( constb^{2}=4\,consta\,constc \). This leads to two possibilities
\[
\begin{array}{l}
abscissa^{2}+abscissa\,ordinate+\frac{1}{4} \,ordinate^{2}-2\,abscissa-ordinate+1=0 \\
abscissa^{2}-abscissa\,ordinate+\frac{1}{4}\,ordinate^{2}-2\,abscissa-ordinate+1=0
\end{array}
\]

Since (1) can be written as \( \left(abscissa+\frac{1}{2} \,ordinate-1\right)^{2}=0 \), we see that it represents a degenerate conic, the double line through the two given points. Hence (2) is the equation of the required parabola.

It is convenient to multiply equation (2) by 4 to eliminate the fraction. We obtain
(3)
\[
4 abscissa^{2}-4 abscissa\,ordinate+ordinate^{2}-8 abscissa-4 ordinate+4=0
\]

Since the quadratic terms in (3) can be written in the form \( (2 abscissa-ordinate)^{2} \), a transformation is suggested. The orthogonal (but not scale-preserving) transformation
\[
\begin{array}{l}
rotatedu=2 abscissa-ordinate \\
rotatedv=abscissa+2 ordinate
\end{array}
\]
with inverse
\[
\begin{array}{l}
abscissa=\frac{1}{5}(2 rotatedu+rotatedv) \\
ordinate=\frac{1}{5}(-rotatedu+2 rotatedv)
\end{array}
\]
transforms (3) into
\[
rotatedu^{2}-\frac{12}{5} rotatedu-\frac{16}{5} rotatedv+4=0
\]

This has the standard form
\[
\left(rotatedu-\frac{6}{5}\right)^{2}-\frac{16}{5}\left(rotatedv-\frac{4}{5}\right)=0
\]

In this form the axis of the parabola is the line \( rotatedu=\frac{6}{5} \), and the vertex has \( rotatedu rotatedv \)-coordinates \( \left(\frac{6}{5}, \frac{4}{5}\right) \). In terms of the original coordinates, the axis has the equation \( 2 abscissa-ordinate=\frac{6}{5} \) and the vertex is at \( \left(\frac{16}{25}, \frac{2}{25}\right) \).

There is another way to handle the first part of this solution.
Suppose two distinct conics are given by the quadratic equations \( funcone(abscissa, ordinate)=0 \) and \( functwo(abscissa, ordinate)=0 \). These conics meet in four points (in the complex projective plane, counting multiplicities) and any other conic passing through these four points has an equation \( scalarlambda\,funcone+scalarmu\,functwo=0 \) for suitable choice of \( scalarlambda \) and \( scalarmu \) (only the ratio \( scalarlambda:scalarmu \) matters, of course). Conics tangent to the \( abscissa \)-axis at \( P \) and tangent to the \( \boldsymbol{ordinate} \)-axis at \( Q \) form such a family because \( P \) and \( Q \) are both double points in this case. Two degenerate conics in this family are \( abscissa\,ordinate=0 \) (the union of the coordinate axes) and \( (2 abscissa+ordinate-2)^{2}=0 \) (the double line \( P Q \) ); consequently all conics of the family have equations of the form
\[
scalarlambda\,abscissa\,ordinate+scalarmu\,(2 abscissa+ordinate-2)^{2}=0 .
\]

Now we determine the parabola in this family by the condition that its discriminant vanishes. We find \( scalarlambda:scalarmu=0 \) or \( scalarlambda:scalarmu=-8 \). The former gives back the double line; and the latter gives the desired parabola.

Second Solution. Let \( \mathcal{P} \) be a parabola with focus \( F \) and directrix \( d \), and suppose two perpendicular lines through \( O \) are tangent to \( \mathcal{P} \) at \( P \) and \( Q \), respectively. Using the well-known reflection property of the parabola, we shall prove synthetically that \( O \) is on \( d \) and \( F \) is the foot of the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \).

Let \( G \) and \( H \) be the reflections of \( F \) in the tangents \( O P \) and \( O Q \), respectively. Then triangles \( P O F \) and \( P O G \) are congruent, as are triangles \( Q O F \) and \( Q O H \). Because \( \angle F P O=\angle G P O \), the ray \( \overrightarrow{F P} \) on reflection from the parabola at \( P \) has the direction opposite to \( \overrightarrow{P G} \). From the reflection property it follows that \( P G \) is parallel to the axis of \( \mathcal{P} \) and perpendicular to \( d \). Therefore, the distance from \( P \) to \( d \) is measured along \( P G \). Since \( P \) is equidistant from \( F \) and \( d \) and \( P F=P G \), we conclude that \( G \) is on \( d \). Similarly, \( H \) is on \( d \).

Now \( \angle G O F=2 \angle P O F \) and \( \angle F O H=2 \angle F O Q \), so \( \angle G O H= 2 \angle P O Q=2 \) right angles, since \( O P \) and \( O Q \) are perpendicular. Hence \( O \) lies on \( G H=d \). Therefore \( \angle O G P \) and \( \angle O H Q \) are right angles. From the original congruent triangles it follows that \( \angle O F P \) and \( \angle O F Q \) are right angles. Hence \( \angle P F Q \) is a straight angle, so \( F \) is at the foot of the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \), as claimed.
In the problem at hand \( O \) is the origin and \( P \) and \( Q \) are the points \( (1,0) \) and ( 0,2 ). The line \( P Q \) has equation \( 2 abscissa+ordinate=2 \) and the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \) has slope \( \frac{1}{2} \). The point \( F \) is found to be \( \left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{2}{5}\right) \). The direction of the axis is found by reflecting the line \( P Q \) in either tangent, so the slope of the axis is 2 . Since it passes through \( F \) the equation of the axis is
\[
5 ordinate=10 abscissa-6
\]

The directrix \( \boldsymbol{d} \) has slope \( -\frac{1}{2} \) and passes through \( O \); hence its equation is \( 2 ordinate+abscissa=0 \). The directrix and axis meet at \( \left(\frac{12}{25}, \frac{6}{25}\right) \), and the vertex is halfway between this point and the focus; that is, at \( \left(\frac{16}{25}, \frac{2}{25}\right) \).

If \( (abscissa, ordinate) \) is any point on the parabola, it is equidistant from the directrix and the focus. Thus, using the squares of these distances
\[
\left(\frac{2 ordinate+abscissa}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^{2}=\left(abscissa-\frac{4}{5}\right)^{2}+\left(ordinate-\frac{2}{5}\right)^{2}
\]

This simplifies to
\[
4 abscissa^{2}-4 abscissa\,ordinate+ordinate^{2}-8 abscissa-4 ordinate+4=0,
\]
the equation of the parabola.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 11. Find the equation of the parabola which touches the \( sunflower \)-axis at the point \( (1,0) \) and the \( toothbrush \)-axis at the point \( (0,2) \). Find the equation of the axis of the parabola and the coordinates of its vertex.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. Clearly the required parabola does not pass through the origin, and any conic not passing through the origin has an equation of the form
\[
chocolate\, sunflower^{2}+cinnamon\, sunflower\, toothbrush+butterfly\, toothbrush^{2}+landscape\, sunflower+pinecone\, toothbrush+1=0 .
\]

In order that this conic be tangent to the \( sunflower \)-axis at \( (1,0) \), the equation obtained by setting \( toothbrush=0 \) must have a double root at \( sunflower=1 \). Hence \( chocolate=1 \) and \( landscape=-2 \). In order that it be tangent to the \( toothbrush \)-axis at \( (0,2) \), we must have \( butterfly=\frac{1}{4},\; pinecone=-1 \). In order that the conic be a parabola we must have \( cinnamon^{2}=4\, chocolate\, butterfly \). This leads to two possibilities
\[
\begin{array}{l}
sunflower^{2}+sunflower\, toothbrush+\frac{1}{4}\, toothbrush^{2}-2\, sunflower- toothbrush+1=0 \\
sunflower^{2}-sunflower\, toothbrush+\frac{1}{4}\, toothbrush^{2}-2\, sunflower- toothbrush+1=0
\end{array}
\]

Since (1) can be written as \( \left(sunflower+\frac{1}{2} toothbrush-1\right)^{2}=0 \), we see that it represents a degenerate conic, the double line through the two given points. Hence (2) is the equation of the required parabola.

It is convenient to multiply equation (2) by 4 to eliminate the fraction. We obtain

\[
4\, sunflower^{2}-4\, sunflower\, toothbrush+toothbrush^{2}-8\, sunflower-4\, toothbrush+4=0
\]

Since the quadratic terms in (3) can be written in the form \( (2\, sunflower-toothbrush)^{2} \), a transformation is suggested. The orthogonal (but not scale-preserving) transformation
\[
\begin{array}{l}
rainstorm=2\, sunflower-toothbrush \\
limestone=sunflower+2\, toothbrush
\end{array}
\]
with inverse
\[
\begin{array}{l}
sunflower=\frac{1}{5}(2\, rainstorm+limestone) \\
toothbrush=\frac{1}{5}(-rainstorm+2\, limestone)
\end{array}
\]
transforms (3) into
\[
rainstorm^{2}-\frac{12}{5}\, rainstorm-\frac{16}{5}\, limestone+4=0
\]

This has the standard form
\[
\left(rainstorm-\frac{6}{5}\right)^{2}-\frac{16}{5}\left(limestone-\frac{4}{5}\right)=0
\]

In this form the axis of the parabola is the line \( rainstorm=\frac{6}{5} \), and the vertex has \( rainstorm\, limestone \)-coordinates \( \left(\frac{6}{5}, \frac{4}{5}\right) \). In terms of the original coordinates, the axis has the equation \( 2\, sunflower-toothbrush=\frac{6}{5} \) and the vertex is at \( \left(\frac{16}{25}, \frac{2}{25}\right) \).

There is another way to handle the first part of this solution.
Suppose two distinct conics are given by the quadratic equations \( drumstick(sunflower, toothbrush)=0 \) and \( paintbrush(sunflower, toothbrush)=0 \). These conics meet in four points (in the complex projective place, counting multiplicities) and any other conic passing through these four points has an equation \( marshmallow\, drumstick+blueberry\, paintbrush=0 \) for suitable choice of \( marshmallow \) and \( blueberry \) (only the ratio \( marshmallow:blueberry \) matters, of course). Conics tangent to the \( sunflower \)-axis at \( P \) and tangent to the \( \boldsymbol{toothbrush} \)-axis at \( Q \) form such a family because \( P \) and \( Q \) are both double points in this case. Two degenerate conics in this family are \( sunflower\, toothbrush=0 \) (the union of the coordinate axes) and \( (2\, sunflower+toothbrush-2)^{2}=0 \) (the double line \( P Q \) ); consequently all conics of the family have equations of the form
\[
marshmallow\, sunflower\, toothbrush+blueberry(2\, sunflower+toothbrush-2)^{2}=0 .
\]

Now we determine the parabola in this family by the condition that its discriminant vanishes. We find \( marshmallow:blueberry=0 \) or \( marshmallow:blueberry=-8 \). The former gives back the double line; and the latter gives the desired parabola.

Second Solution. Let \( \mathcal{P} \) be a parabola with focus \( F \) and directrix \( d \), and suppose two perpendicular lines through \( O \) are tangent to \( \mathcal{P} \) at \( P \) and \( Q \), respectively. Then triangles \( P O F \) and \( P O G \) are congruent, as are triangles \( Q O F \) and \( Q O H \). Because \( \angle F P O=\angle G P O \), the ray \( \overrightarrow{F P} \) on reflection from the parabola at \( P \) has the direction opposite to \( \overrightarrow{P G} \). From the reflection property it follows that \( P G \) is parallel to the axis of \( \mathcal{P} \) and perpendicular to \( d \). Therefore, the distance from \( P \) to \( d \) is measured along \( P G \). Since \( P \) is equidistant from \( F \) and \( d \) and \( P F=P G \), we conclude that \( G \) is on \( d \). Similarly, \( H \) is on \( d \).

Now \( \angle G O F=2 \angle P O F \) and \( \angle F O H=2 \angle F O Q \), so \( \angle G O H=2 \angle P O Q=2 \) right angles, since \( O P \) and \( O Q \) are perpendicular. Hence \( O \) lies on \( G H=d \). Therefore \( \angle O G P \) and \( \angle O H Q \) are right angles. Hence \( \angle P F Q \) is a straight angle, so \( F \) is at the foot of the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \), as claimed.
In the problem at hand \( O \) is the origin and \( P \) and \( Q \) are the points \( (1,0) \) and \( (0,2) \). The line \( P Q \) has equation \( 2\, sunflower+toothbrush=2 \) and the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \) has slope \( \frac{1}{2} \). The point \( F \) is found to be \( \left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{2}{5}\right) \). The direction of the axis is found by reflecting the line \( P Q \) in either tangent, so the slope of the axis is 2. Since it passes through \( F \) the equation of the axis is
\[
5\, toothbrush=10\, sunflower-6
\]

The directrix \( \boldsymbol{d} \) has slope \( -\frac{1}{2} \) and passes through \( O \); hence its equation is \( 2\, toothbrush+sunflower=0 \). The directrix and axis meet at \( \left(\frac{12}{25}, \frac{6}{25}\right) \), and the vertex is halfway between this point and the focus; that is, at \( \left(\frac{16}{25}, \frac{2}{25}\right) \).

\[
\left(\frac{2\, toothbrush+sunflower}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^{2}=\left(sunflower-\frac{4}{5}\right)^{2}+\left(toothbrush-\frac{2}{5}\right)^{2}
\]

This simplifies to
\[
4\, sunflower^{2}-4\, sunflower\, toothbrush+toothbrush^{2}-8\, sunflower-4\, toothbrush+4=0,
\]
the equation of the parabola.

Using the well-known reflection property of the parabola, we shall prove synthetically that \( O \) is on \( d \) and \( F \) is the foot of the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \).

Let \( G \) and \( H \) be the reflections of \( F \) in the tangents \( O P \) and \( O Q \), respectively. Then triangles \( P O F \) and \( P O G \) are congruent, as are triangles \( Q O F \) and \( Q O H \). Because \( \angle F P O=\angle G P O \), the ray \( \overrightarrow{F P} \) on reflection from the parabola at \( P \) has the direction opposite to \( \overrightarrow{P G} \). From the reflection property it follows that \( P G \) is parallel to the axis of \( \mathcal{P} \) and perpendicular to \( d \). Therefore, the distance from \( P \) to \( d \) is measured along \( P G \). Since \( P \) is equidistant from \( F \) and \( d \) and \( P F=P G \), we conclude that \( G \) is on \( d \). Similarly, \( H \) is on \( d \).

Now \( \angle G O F=2 \angle P O F \) and \( \angle F O H=2 \angle F O Q \), so \( \angle G O H=2 \angle P O Q=2 \) right angles, since \( O P \) and \( O Q \) are perpendicular. Hence \( O \) lies on \( G H=d \). Therefore \( \angle O G P \) and \( \angle O H Q \) are right angles. From the original congruent triangles it follows that \( \angle O F P \) and \( \angle O F Q \) are right angles. Hence \( \angle P F Q \) is a straight angle, so \( F \) is at the foot of the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \), as claimed.

In the problem at hand \( O \) is the origin and \( P \) and \( Q \) are the points \( (1,0) \) and \( (0,2) \). The line \( P Q \) has equation \( 2\, verticalaxis + horizontalaxis =2 \) and the perpendicular from \( O \) on \( P Q \) has slope \( \frac{1}{2} \). The point \( F \) is found to be \( \left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{2}{5}\right) \). The direction of the axis is found by reflecting the line \( P Q \) in either tangent, so the slope of the axis is 2 . Since it passes through \( F \) the equation of the axis is
\[
5 \, horizontalaxis =10 \, verticalaxis -6
\]

The directrix \( d \) has slope \( -\frac{1}{2} \) and passes through \( O \); hence its equation is \( 2 \, horizontalaxis + verticalaxis =0 \). The directrix and axis meet at \( \left(\frac{12}{25}, \frac{6}{25}\right) \), and the vertex is halfway between this point and the focus; that is, at \( \left(\frac{16}{25}, \frac{2}{25}\right) \).

If \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \) is any point on the parabola, it is equidistant from the directrix and the focus. Thus, using the squares of these distances
\[
\left(\frac{2 \, horizontalaxis + verticalaxis}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^{2}=\left(verticalaxis - \frac{4}{5}\right)^{2}+\left(horizontalaxis - \frac{2}{5}\right)^{2}
\]

This simplifies to
\[
4 \, verticalaxis^{2}-4 \, verticalaxis \, horizontalaxis + horizontalaxis^{2}-8 \, verticalaxis -4 \, horizontalaxis +4=0,
\]
the equation of the parabola.

garbled_string.question: Find the equation of the parabola which touches the \( qzxwvtnp \)-axis at the point \((1,0)\) and the \( hjgrksla \)-axis at the point \((0,2)\). Find the equation of the axis of the parabola and the coordinates of its vertex.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. Clearly the required parabola does not pass through the origin, and any conic not passing through the origin has an equation of the form
\[
lwcxfprg\,qzxwvtnp^{2}+zhdktqop\,qzxwvtnp\,hjgrksla+nvsrqmji\,hjgrksla^{2}+ylfwzbea\,qzxwvtnp+kbnhsmcd\,hjgrksla+1=0 .
\]
In order that this conic be tangent to the \( qzxwvtnp \)-axis at \((1,0)\), the equation obtained by setting \( hjgrksla=0 \) must have a double root at \( qzxwvtnp=1 \). Hence \( lwcxfprg=1 \) and \( ylfwzbea=-2 \). In order that it be tangent to the \( hjgrksla \)-axis at \((0,2)\), we must have \( nvsrqmji=\tfrac{1}{4},\ kbnhsmcd=-1 \). For the conic to be a parabola we must have \( zhdktqop^{2}=4\,lwcxfprg\,nvsrqmji \), giving two possibilities
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp^{2}+qzxwvtnp\,hjgrksla+\tfrac14 hjgrksla^{2}-2qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla+1=0\\[2pt]
qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp\,hjgrksla+\tfrac14 hjgrksla^{2}-2qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla+1=0
\end{array}
\]
Since (1) can be written \( (qzxwvtnp+\tfrac12 hjgrksla-1)^{2}=0 \), it is a degenerate double line, so (2) is the required parabola.  Multiplying (2) by 4 gives
\[
4qzxwvtnp^{2}-4qzxwvtnp\,hjgrksla+hjgrksla^{2}-8qzxwvtnp-4hjgrksla+4=0\tag{3}
\]
Because the quadratic part is \((2qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2}\), set
\[
\begin{array}{l}
mndtckle=2qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla,\\
prskbjwe=qzxwvtnp+2hjgrksla,
\end{array}\qquad
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp=\tfrac15(2mndtckle+prskbjwe),\\
hjgrksla=\tfrac15(-mndtckle+2prskbjwe).
\end{array}
\]
Then (3) becomes
\[
mndtckle^{2}-\tfrac{12}{5}mndtckle-\tfrac{16}{5}prskbjwe+4=0,
\]
that is
\[
\bigl(mndtckle-\tfrac65\bigr)^{2}-\tfrac{16}{5}\bigl(prskbjwe-\tfrac45\bigr)=0.
\]
Hence the axis is \( mndtckle=\tfrac65 \) and the vertex has \( mndtckle\,prskbjwe \)-coordinates \( (\tfrac65,\tfrac45) \).  Returning to \( qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla \) gives the axis \( 2qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla=\tfrac65 \) and vertex \( (\tfrac{16}{25},\tfrac{2}{25}) \).

Another approach: let two distinct conics be given by \( rgdxqluo(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla)=0 \) and \( xwpkjfai(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla)=0 \).  Any other conic through their four (possibly complex) intersection points has equation \( uoadzvhe\,rgdxqluo+sibqartm\,xwpkjfai=0 \) (only the ratio \( uoadzvhe:sibqartm \) matters).  Conics tangent to the coordinate axes at the given points form such a family; two degenerate members are \( qzxwvtnp hjgrksla=0 \) and \( (2qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla-2)^{2}=0 \).  Thus every member satisfies
\[
uoadzvhe\,qzxwvtnp hjgrksla+sibqartm(2qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla-2)^{2}=0.
\]
The parabola is characterized by vanishing discriminant, giving \( uoadzvhe:sibqartm=0 \) (the double line) or \( uoadzvhe:sibqartm=-8 \) (the desired parabola).

Second Solution.  Let \( \mathcal P \) have focus \( F \) and directrix \( d \).  If two perpendicular tangents through \( O \) touch \( \mathcal P \) at \( P,Q \), reflecting \( F \) in the tangents shows that \( O\in d \) and that \( F \) is the foot of the perpendicular from \( O \) to \( P Q \).

In our problem \( O \) is the origin and \( P=(1,0),\ Q=(0,2) \).  The line \( P Q \) is \( 2qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla=2 \); the perpendicular from \( O \) to \( P Q \) has slope \( \tfrac12 \), giving \( F=(\tfrac45,\tfrac25) \).  Reflecting \( P Q \) in a tangent shows the axis has slope 2, so through \( F \) it is
\[
5hjgrksla=10qzxwvtnp-6.
\]
The directrix has slope \(-\tfrac12\) and passes through the origin: \( 2hjgrksla+qzxwvtnp=0 \).  The axis and directrix meet at \( (\tfrac{12}{25},\tfrac{6}{25}) \); the vertex, midway to the focus, is \( (\tfrac{16}{25},\tfrac{2}{25}) \).

For any point \((qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla)\) on the parabola the squared distances to focus and directrix are equal:
\[
\Bigl(\tfrac{2hjgrksla+qzxwvtnp}{\sqrt5}\Bigr)^{2}=\Bigl(qzxwvtnp-\tfrac45\Bigr)^{2}+\Bigl(hjgrksla-\tfrac25\Bigr)^{2},
\]
which simplifies to
\[
4qzxwvtnp^{2}-4qzxwvtnp\,hjgrksla+hjgrksla^{2}-8qzxwvtnp-4hjgrksla+4=0,
\]
the required equation.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $\mathcal{S}\subset\mathbb{R}^{3}$ be a non-degenerate quadric surface that fulfils the six requirements below.  

1. (Axis) $\mathcal{S}$ is a paraboloid of revolution whose axis is the line  
\[
\ell:\;\mathbf r(t)=t(1,1,1)\qquad(t\in\mathbb{R}).
\]

2. (Vertex-tangent plane) The vertex $V$ of $\mathcal{S}$ lies on, and is tangent to, the plane  
\[
\Pi:\;x+y+z=1 .
\]

3. (Rotational symmetry) $\mathcal{S}$ is rotationally symmetric about $\ell$.

4. (First planar section) In the plane $z=0$ the intersection  
\[
\varepsilon_{1}:=\mathcal{S}\cap\{z=0\}
\]
is an ellipse that  
(i) is tangent to the $x$-axis at the point $A(2,0,0)$, and  
(ii) has its \emph{major} axis parallel to the vector $(1,1,0)$.

5. (Second planar section) In the plane $x=0$ the intersection  
\[
\varepsilon_{2}:=\mathcal{S}\cap\{x=0\}
\]
is an ellipse that  
(i) is tangent to the $z$-axis at the point $B(0,0,2)$, and  
(ii) has its \emph{major} axis parallel to the vector $(0,1,1)$.

6. (Location of the focus) The focus $F$ of $\mathcal{S}$ lies on the plane  
\[
\sigma:\;x-2y+z=0 .
\]

(a) Prove that the six geometric requirements determine a unique quadric surface.  

(b) Determine its Cartesian equation in the form  
\[
Q(x,y,z)=Ax^{2}+By^{2}+Cz^{2}+Dxy+Eyz+Fzx+Gx+Hy+Iz+1=0,
\]
and list the nine coefficients $A,\dots ,I$.  

(c) Find the focus $F$ and the directrix $\delta$ of $\mathcal{S}$.  

(d) Rigorously verify that your surface satisfies the six requirements.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout bold lower-case letters denote vectors and plain letters their coordinates in the standard basis $(\mathbf e_{x},\mathbf e_{y},\mathbf e_{z})$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.  An orthonormal frame adapted to the axis
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Put  
\[
\mathbf e_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt 3}(1,1,1),\qquad 
\mathbf e_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt 2}(1,-1,0),\qquad
\mathbf e_{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt 6}(1,1,-2).
\]
Then $\mathbf e_{1}$ is directed along the axis $\ell$ and
$\{\mathbf e_{1},\mathbf e_{2},\mathbf e_{3}\}$ is an orthonormal basis of
$\mathbb{R}^{3}$.

For every point $X=(x,y,z)$ define the coordinates
\[
u=\mathbf e_{1}\cdot X=\frac{x+y+z}{\sqrt 3},\quad
v=\mathbf e_{2}\cdot X=\frac{x-y}{\sqrt 2},\quad
w=\mathbf e_{3}\cdot X=\frac{x+y-2z}{\sqrt 6},
\tag{1}
\]
and conversely  
\[
\begin{aligned}
x&=\frac{u}{\sqrt 3}+\frac{v}{\sqrt 2}+\frac{w}{\sqrt 6},\\
y&=\frac{u}{\sqrt 3}-\frac{v}{\sqrt 2}+\frac{w}{\sqrt 6},\\
z&=\frac{u}{\sqrt 3}-\frac{2w}{\sqrt 6}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{2}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
2.  Canonical equation in $(u,v,w)$-coordinates
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Because of rotational symmetry about the $u$-axis (Requirement 3)
\[
u=\alpha\bigl(v^{2}+w^{2}\bigr)+\beta , \qquad\alpha\neq 0.
\tag{3}
\]
The sign of $\alpha$ will be settled in Step 7.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
3.  First planar section $z=0$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Setting $z=0$ in (1) gives
\[
u=\frac{x+y}{\sqrt 3},\quad
v=\frac{x-y}{\sqrt 2},\quad
w=\frac{x+y}{\sqrt 6},
\]
whence
\[
v^{2}+w^{2}=\frac{(x-y)^{2}}{2}+\frac{(x+y)^{2}}{6}
           =\frac{2}{3}\bigl(x^{2}-xy+y^{2}\bigr).
\tag{4}
\]
Substituting (4) into (3) and clearing denominators we obtain inside the
plane $z=0$
\[
k\bigl(x^{2}-xy+y^{2}\bigr)-(x+y)+\beta\sqrt 3=0,
\qquad k:=\frac{2\alpha\sqrt 3}{3}.
\tag{5}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
4.  Tangency at $A(2,0,0)$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
For the point $A$ equation (1) yields
\[
u_{A}=\frac{2}{\sqrt 3},\qquad
v_{A}=\sqrt 2,\qquad
w_{A}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}.
\tag{6}
\]
Requirement 4(i) says $A\in\mathcal{S}$, hence (3) gives
\[
\frac{2}{\sqrt 3}=\alpha\cdot\frac83+\beta
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\beta=\frac{2}{\sqrt 3}-\frac{8}{3}\alpha .
\tag{7}
\]

The $x$-component of the gradient must vanish at $A$ because of
tangency to the $x$-axis.
Differentiating the implicit equation
$\Phi(u,v,w):=u-\alpha(v^{2}+w^{2})-\beta=0$
gives
\[
\nabla\Phi=\mathbf e_{1}-2\alpha\bigl(v\,\mathbf e_{2}+w\,\mathbf e_{3}\bigr).
\]
Consequently
\[
0=n_{x}(A)
  =\Bigl(\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\nabla\Phi\Bigr)_{A}
  =\frac1{\sqrt 3}-2\alpha
     \Bigl(v_{A}\,\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\mathbf e_{2}
           +w_{A}\,\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\mathbf e_{3}\Bigr).
\]
Using $\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\mathbf e_{2}=1/\sqrt 2$ and
$\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\mathbf e_{3}=1/\sqrt 6$ together with (6) we obtain
\[
\frac1{\sqrt 3}-\frac{16}{3}\alpha=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\alpha=\frac{3}{8\sqrt 3}.
\tag{8}
\]
With (8) the value of $\beta$ in (7) becomes
\[
\beta=\frac1{\sqrt 3}.
\tag{9}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
5.  Second planar section $x=0$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
At $B(0,0,2)$ one finds from (1)
\[
u_{B}=\frac{2}{\sqrt 3},\qquad
v_{B}=0,\qquad
w_{B}=-\frac{4}{\sqrt 6}.
\tag{10}
\]
Because $v_{B}^{2}+w_{B}^{2}=8/3$, equation (3) with (8)-(9) is
satisfied, so $B\in\mathcal{S}$ (Requirement 5(i)).  

The gradient at $B$ equals
\[
\nabla\Phi(B)=\mathbf e_{1}-2\alpha\,w_{B}\mathbf e_{3}.
\]
Using $-2\alpha w_{B}=+1/\sqrt 2$, one gets  
\[
\begin{aligned}
n_{z}(B)&=\mathbf e_{z}\cdot\nabla\Phi(B)
        =\frac1{\sqrt 3}+\frac1{\sqrt 2}\,\mathbf e_{z}\cdot\mathbf e_{3}
        =\frac1{\sqrt 3}-\frac2{\sqrt{12}}=0,\\[4pt]
n_{y}(B)&=\mathbf e_{y}\cdot\nabla\Phi(B)
        =\frac1{\sqrt 3}+\frac1{\sqrt 2}\,\mathbf e_{y}\cdot\mathbf e_{3}
        =\frac1{\sqrt 3}+\frac1{\sqrt{12}}
        =\frac{3}{2\sqrt 3}=\frac{\sqrt 3}{2}\neq 0 .
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence the tangent line to $\varepsilon_{2}$ at $B$ is parallel to the
$z$-axis, fulfilling Requirement 5(i).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
6.  Vertex and tangent plane $\Pi$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Rotational symmetry forces $v=w=0$ at the vertex, hence $u=\beta$.
With (9) the vertex is
\[
V=\beta\,\mathbf e_{1}=\Bigl(\tfrac13,\tfrac13,\tfrac13\Bigr).
\]
The tangent plane $\mathbf e_{1}\cdot(X-V)=0$ is
$x+y+z=1$, verifying Requirement 2.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
7.  Sign of $\alpha$ and uniqueness
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
If $\alpha<0$ the surface opens in the direction opposite to that
prescribed by the tangency data (intersections $\varepsilon_{1},
\varepsilon_{2}$ would lie on the ``concave'' side of the vertex).
Hence $\alpha>0$ is forced, and the numerical values
(8)-(9) are uniquely determined.

Notice that Requirements $1$-$5$ already single out a \emph{unique}
quadric.  After this unique candidate is constructed we shall check
that its focus indeed lies on $\sigma$ (Requirement $6$); thus the sixth
requirement is \emph{automatically} satisfied and could be omitted
without affecting uniqueness.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
8.  Focus and directrix
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
In $(u,v,w)$-coordinates the generating parabola \eqref{3} has focal
length $p=1/(4\alpha)$, so its focus is
\[
F'=\bigl(\beta+p,\,0,\,0\bigr)
   =\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt 3}+\tfrac{2\sqrt 3}{3},\,0,\,0\Bigr)
   =( \sqrt 3,0,0).
\]
Using (2) this becomes
\[
F=(1,1,1).
\tag{11}
\]
Requirement 6 is met because $1-2\cdot1+1=0$.

The directrix, perpendicular to $\ell$ and situated a distance $p$
below the vertex along $\ell$, is
\[
\delta:\;x+y+z=-1 .
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
9.  Cartesian equation of $\mathcal{S}$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Insert the inverse relations (2) into (3), with the numerical values
(8)-(9), and multiply by $24\sqrt 3$:
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-xy-yz-zx-4(x+y+z)+4=0.
\tag{12}
\]
Because $Q$ is required to have constant term $+1$, divide by $4$ to get
\[
\boxed{\;
   \tfrac14\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-xy-yz-zx\bigr)
   -(x+y+z)+1=0
\;}
\tag{13}
\]
and read off
\[
A=B=C=\tfrac14,\quad
D=E=F=-\tfrac14,\quad
G=H=I=-1.
\tag{14}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
10.  Verification of Requirements 4(ii) and 5(ii)
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
(i)  Section $z=0$.  
Substituting $z=0$ in (13) yields
\[
\tfrac14\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}-xy\bigr)-x-y+1=0.
\tag{15}
\]
Its centre is $(4,4)$, found by solving
$\partial Q/\partial x=\partial Q/\partial y=0$.
Writing $X:=x-4,\;Y:=y-4$ one obtains
\[
\frac{1}{12}\bigl(X^{2}+Y^{2}-XY\bigr)=1.
\tag{16}
\]
The quadratic part has matrix
$M=\begin{pmatrix}1&-\tfrac12\\ -\tfrac12&1\end{pmatrix}$ with
eigen-data
\[
\lambda_{1}=0.5,\; \mathbf v_{1}=(1,1),\qquad
\lambda_{2}=1.5,\; \mathbf v_{2}=(1,-1).
\]
Because the semi-axis lengths are
$a_{i}=1/\sqrt{12\lambda_{i}}$,
the \emph{longer} (major) axis is parallel to $\mathbf v_{1}$,
that is, to $(1,1,0)$, while the \emph{shorter} (minor) axis
is parallel to $(1,-1,0)$.
Hence Requirement 4(ii) is satisfied.

(ii)  Section $x=0$.  
Putting $x=0$ in (13) gives
\[
\tfrac14\bigl(y^{2}+z^{2}-yz\bigr)-y-z+1=0.
\tag{17}
\]
Its centre is $(4,4)$ in $(y,z)$, and in shifted coordinates
$Y:=y-4,\;Z:=z-4$ one has
\[
\frac{1}{12}\bigl(Y^{2}+Z^{2}-YZ\bigr)=1.
\]
The same matrix $M$ occurs, now acting on $(Y,Z)$, so the major axis
is parallel to $(1,1)$ in the $(y,z)$-plane, that is, to
$(0,1,1)$ in $\mathbb{R}^{3}$, as demanded by Requirement 5(ii).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Answer to the four items
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
(a)  The conditions force $\alpha>0$ and uniquely determine all
numerical constants; hence exactly one quadric satisfies Requirements
$1$-$5$, and this quadric automatically fulfils Requirement $6$ as well.

(b)  The Cartesian equation is
\[
\frac14\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-xy-yz-zx\bigr)-(x+y+z)+1=0,
\]
with
\[
A=B=C=\tfrac14,\quad
D=E=F=-\tfrac14,\quad
G=H=I=-1.
\]

(c)  Focus $F(1,1,1)$, directrix $\delta:\;x+y+z=-1$.

(d)  Step-by-step verification has been supplied in Sections $3$-$10$.

Throughout bold lower-case letters denote vectors and plain letters their coordinates in the standard basis $(\mathbf e_{x},\mathbf e_{y},\mathbf e_{z})$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.  An orthonormal frame adapted to the axis
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\[

v=\mathbf e_{2}\cdot X=\frac{x-y}{\sqrt 2},\quad
w=\mathbf e_{3}\cdot X=\frac{x+y-2z}{\sqrt 6},
\tag{1}
\]
and conversely  
\[
\begin{aligned}
x&=\frac{u}{\sqrt 3}+\frac{v}{\sqrt 2}+\frac{w}{\sqrt 6},\\
y&=\frac{u}{\sqrt 3}-\frac{v}{\sqrt 2}+\frac{w}{\sqrt 6},\\
z&=\frac{u}{\sqrt 3}-\frac{2w}{\sqrt 6}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{2}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
2.  Canonical equation in $(u,v,w)$-coordinates
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\[
u=\alpha\bigl(v^{2}+w^{2}\bigr)+\beta , \qquad\alpha\neq 0.
\tag{3}
\]
The sign of $\alpha$ will be settled in Step 7.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
3.  First planar section $z=0$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Setting $z=0$ in (1) gives
\[
u=\frac{x+y}{\sqrt 3},\quad
v=\frac{x-y}{\sqrt 2},\quad
w=\frac{x+y}{\sqrt 6},
\]
whence
\[
v^{2}+w^{2}=\frac{(x-y)^{2}}{2}+\frac{(x+y)^{2}}{6}
           =\frac{2}{3}\bigl(x^{2}-xy+y^{2}\bigr).
\tag{4}
\]
Substituting (4) into (3) and clearing denominators we obtain inside the
plane $z=0$
\[
k\bigl(x^{2}-xy+y^{2}\bigr)-(x+y)+\beta\sqrt 3=0,
\qquad k:=\frac{2\alpha\sqrt 3}{3}.
\tag{5}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
4.  Tangency at $A(2,0,0)$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
For the point $A$ equation (1) yields
\[
u_{A}=\frac{2}{\sqrt 3},\qquad
v_{A}=\sqrt 2,\qquad
w_{A}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}.
\tag{6}
\]
Requirement 4(i) says $A\in\mathcal{S}$, hence (3) gives
\[
\frac{2}{\sqrt 3}=\alpha\cdot\frac83+\beta
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\beta=\frac{2}{\sqrt 3}-\frac{8}{3}\alpha .
\tag{7}
\]

The $x$-component of the gradient must vanish at $A$ because of
tangency to the $x$-axis.
Differentiating the implicit equation
$\Phi(u,v,w):=u-\alpha(v^{2}+w^{2})-\beta=0$
gives
\[
\nabla\Phi=\mathbf e_{1}-2\alpha\bigl(v\,\mathbf e_{2}+w\,\mathbf e_{3}\bigr).
\]
Consequently
\[
0=n_{x}(A)
  =\Bigl(\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\nabla\Phi\Bigr)_{A}
  =\frac1{\sqrt 3}-2\alpha
     \Bigl(v_{A}\,\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\mathbf e_{2}
           +w_{A}\,\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\mathbf e_{3}\Bigr).
\]
Using $\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\mathbf e_{2}=1/\sqrt 2$ and
$\mathbf e_{x}\cdot\mathbf e_{3}=1/\sqrt 6$ together with (6) we obtain
\[
\frac1{\sqrt 3}-\frac{16}{3}\alpha=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\alpha=\frac{3}{8\sqrt 3}.
\tag{8}
\]
With (8) the value of $\beta$ in (7) becomes
\[
\beta=\frac1{\sqrt 3}.
\tag{9}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
5.  Second planar section $x=0$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
At $B(0,0,2)$ one finds from (1)
\[
u_{B}=\frac{2}{\sqrt 3},\qquad
v_{B}=0,\qquad
w_{B}=-\frac{4}{\sqrt 6}.
\tag{10}
\]
Because $v_{B}^{2}+w_{B}^{2}=8/3$, equation (3) with (8)-(9) is
satisfied, so $B\in\mathcal{S}$ (Requirement 5(i)).  

The gradient at $B$ equals
\[
\nabla\Phi(B)=\mathbf e_{1}-2\alpha\,w_{B}\mathbf e_{3},
\]
and since $\mathbf e_{z}\cdot\mathbf e_{3}=-2/\sqrt 6$ one obtains
\[
n_{z}(B)=\mathbf e_{z}\cdot\nabla\Phi(B)=0 .
\]
The remaining in-plane component
$\mathbf e_{y}\cdot\nabla\Phi(B)=-\tfrac32\neq 0$
shows that the tangent line coincides with the $z$-axis, fulfilling
Requirement 5(i).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
6.  Vertex and tangent plane $\Pi$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Rotational symmetry forces $v=w=0$ at the vertex, hence $u=\beta$.
With (9) the vertex is
\[
V=\beta\,\mathbf e_{1}=\Bigl(\tfrac13,\tfrac13,\tfrac13\Bigr).
\]
The tangent plane $\mathbf e_{1}\cdot(X-V)=0$ is
$x+y+z=1$, verifying Requirement 2.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
7.  Sign of $\alpha$ and uniqueness
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
If $\alpha<0$ the surface opens in the direction opposite to that
prescribed by the tangency data (intersections $\varepsilon_{1},
\varepsilon_{2}$ would lie on the ``concave'' side of the vertex).
Hence $\alpha>0$ is forced, and the numerical values
(8)-(9) are uniquely determined.
Therefore \emph{there is exactly one} quadric fulfilling the six
requirements, proving part (a).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
8.  Focus and directrix
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
In $(u,v,w)$-coordinates the generating parabola \eqref{3} has focal
length $p=1/(4\alpha)$, so its focus is
\[
F'=\bigl(\beta+p,\,0,\,0\bigr)
   =\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt 3}+\tfrac{2\sqrt 3}{3},\,0,\,0\Bigr)
   =( \sqrt 3,0,0).
\]
Using (2) this becomes
\[
F=(1,1,1).
\tag{11}
\]
Requirement 6 is met because $1-2\cdot1+1=0$.

The directrix, perpendicular to $\ell$ and situated a distance $p$
below the vertex along $\ell$, is
\[
\delta:\;x+y+z=-1 .
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
9.  Cartesian equation of $\mathcal{S}$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Insert the inverse relations (2) into (3), with the numerical values
(8)-(9), and multiply by $24\sqrt 3$:
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-xy-yz-zx-4(x+y+z)+4=0.
\tag{12}
\]
Because $Q$ is required to have constant term $+1$, divide by $4$ to get
\[
\boxed{\;
   \tfrac14\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-xy-yz-zx\bigr)
   -(x+y+z)+1=0
\;}
\tag{13}
\]
and read off
\[
A=B=C=\tfrac14,\quad
D=E=F=-\tfrac14,\quad
G=H=I=-1.
\tag{14}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
10.  Verification of Requirements 4(ii) and 5(ii)
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
(i)  Section $z=0$.  
Substituting $z=0$ in (13) yields
\[
\tfrac14\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}-xy\bigr)-x-y+1=0.
\tag{15}
\]
Its centre is $(4,4)$, found by solving
$\partial Q/\partial x=\partial Q/\partial y=0$.
Writing $X:=x-4,\;Y:=y-4$ one obtains
\[
\frac{1}{12}\bigl(X^{2}+Y^{2}-XY\bigr)=1.
\tag{16}
\]
The quadratic part has matrix
$M=\begin{pmatrix}1&-\tfrac12\\ -\tfrac12&1\end{pmatrix}$ with
eigen-data
\[
\lambda_{1}=0.5,\; \mathbf v_{1}=(1,1),\qquad
\lambda_{2}=1.5,\; \mathbf v_{2}=(1,-1).
\]
Because the semi-axis lengths are
$a_{i}=1/\sqrt{12\lambda_{i}}$,
the \emph{longer} (major) axis is parallel to $\mathbf v_{1}$,
that is, to $(1,1,0)$, while the \emph{shorter} (minor) axis
is parallel to $(1,-1,0)$.
Hence Requirement 4(ii) is satisfied.

(ii)  Section $x=0$.  
Putting $x=0$ in (13) gives
\[
\tfrac14\bigl(y^{2}+z^{2}-yz\bigr)-y-z+1=0.
\tag{17}
\]
Its centre is $(4,4)$ in $(y,z)$, and in shifted coordinates
$Y:=y-4,\;Z:=z-4$ one has
\[
\frac{1}{12}\bigl(Y^{2}+Z^{2}-YZ\bigr)=1.
\]
The same matrix $M$ occurs, now acting on $(Y,Z)$, so the major axis
is parallel to $(1,1)$ in the $(y,z)$-plane, that is, to
$(0,1,1)$ in $\mathbb{R}^{3}$, as demanded by Requirement 5(ii).

All six requirements are therefore met.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Answer to the four items
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
(a)  The conditions force $\alpha>0$ and uniquely determine all
numerical constants; hence exactly one quadric satisfies them.

(b)  The Cartesian equation is
\[
\frac14\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-xy-yz-zx\bigr)-(x+y+z)+1=0,
\]
with
\[
A=B=C=\tfrac14,\quad
D=E=F=-\tfrac14,\quad
G=H=I=-1.
\]

(c)  Focus $F(1,1,1)$, directrix $\delta:\;x+y+z=-1$.

(d)  Step-by-step verification has been supplied in Sections 3-10.
